content:
# Leviticus 16 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Leviticus 16 (34 verses) — Day of Atonement
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Agents:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic)
**Total disagreements:** 42

---

## Summary

Leviticus 16 is the longest and most theologically dense chapter yet arbited, and it generates the highest disagreement count in the Leviticus series. The three agents share broad structural agreement — all preserve Azazel untranslated, all use kapporet (though PNEUMA uses "mercy seat"), all render chattat as "sin offering" and olah as "burnt offering." The disagreements cluster in five main areas:

1. **Name forms and address formulas** (EMET: Moshe/Aharon/Yisra'el throughout vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA English forms).
2. **Kapporet vs. "mercy seat"** — PNEUMA departs from the project ruling and uses "mercy seat" throughout, with "Most Holy Place" substituted for "Holy Place" in multiple verses.
3. **Vv. 29, 31 — "afflict yourselves" vs. "humble yourselves"** — the Yom Kippur self-denial formula, the chapter's most important repeated term, splits PNEUMA (following the LXX) against EMET and LOGOS.
4. **"Uncleannesses" vs. "ritual impurities"** — PNEUMA's interpretive addition of "ritual" throughout.
5. **The three-term sin catalogue** — all three agents render all three terms but PNEUMA substitutes "acts of rebellion" for the project standard "transgressions."

The most consequential disagreement is on kapporet/mercy seat: PNEUMA's choice of "mercy seat" throughout is theologically defensible (it is the KJV/traditional rendering) but violates the project ruling to preserve kapporet as a transliteration. A secondary significant disagreement is the "afflict/humble" split in vv. 29 and 31, which determines whether readers understand Yom Kippur self-denial as bodily discipline (MT emphasis) or interior humility (LXX/PNEUMA emphasis).

---

## Disagreement 1 — V. 1: Name form "Moshe" vs. "Moses"

**Verse:** 1
**Hebrew:** מֹשֶׁה (Moshe)
**EMET:** "Moshe"
**LOGOS:** "Moses"
**PNEUMA:** "Moses"
**Ruling:** "Moses"
**Type:** Name convention
**Notes:** EMET maintains Hebrew transliteration throughout. LOGOS and PNEUMA use the English form. Project convention (established from Genesis onward): English name forms in the consensus text. This disagreement recurs at vv. 2, 34 wherever Moses is named; it is logged once here and applies throughout the chapter.

---

## Disagreement 2 — V. 1: Name form "Aharon" vs. "Aaron"

**Verse:** 1 (and throughout)
**Hebrew:** אַהֲרֹן (Aharon)
**EMET:** "Aharon"
**LOGOS:** "Aaron"
**PNEUMA:** "Aaron"
**Ruling:** "Aaron"
**Type:** Name convention
**Notes:** Same rule as Disagreement 1. EMET's transliteration is consistent with its literal register; English form in consensus. Applies throughout all verses where Aaron is named.

---

## Disagreement 3 — V. 1: "Yisra'el" vs. "Israel"

**Verse:** 5, 16, 17, 19, 21, 29, 34 (all instances)
**Hebrew:** יִשְׂרָאֵל (Yisra'el)
**EMET:** "Yisra'el"
**LOGOS:** "Israel"
**PNEUMA:** "Israel" / "Israelites"
**Ruling:** "Israel" in the nation-name; "sons of Israel" for bney yisrael
**Type:** Name convention
**Notes:** EMET transliterates consistently. PNEUMA at several points uses "Israelites" (vv. 16, 17, 19, 21, 27) for bney yisrael rather than "sons of Israel." Ruling: "sons of Israel" for bney yisrael per project convention; "Israel" for the nation name alone. PNEUMA's "Israelites" is a legitimate dynamic rendering but departs from the project standard that retains the familial/generational resonance of bney.

---

## Disagreement 4 — V. 1: "the LORD's presence" vs. "before the LORD"

**Verse:** 1
**Hebrew:** לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (lifnei YHWH)
**EMET:** "before the LORD"
**LOGOS:** "before the LORD"
**PNEUMA:** "the LORD's presence"
**Ruling:** "before the LORD"
**Type:** Word choice / cultic formula
**Notes:** PNEUMA paraphrases the cultic spatial formula lifnei YHWH ("before the face of YHWH") as "the LORD's presence." This phrase is a technical cultic expression throughout the Tabernacle literature (occurring approximately 40 times in Leviticus alone); it should be rendered literally. "The LORD's presence" is the correct referent but interprets rather than translates.

---

## Disagreement 5 — V. 2: "Speak" vs. "Tell"

**Verse:** 2
**Hebrew:** דַּבֵּר (dabber, Piel imperative of dabar)
**EMET:** "Speak"
**LOGOS:** "Speak"
**PNEUMA:** "Tell"
**Ruling:** "Speak"
**Type:** Word choice / register
**Notes:** The Piel imperative dabber is the standard prophetic-legal speech command throughout Leviticus (appearing in this form at 1:2, 4:2, 7:23, 11:2, etc.). PNEUMA's "tell" is conversational; "speak" preserves the formal address register of the text.

---

## Disagreement 6 — V. 2: "at any time" vs. "at all times" vs. "whenever he chooses"

**Verse:** 2
**Hebrew:** בְּכָל-עֵת (be-khol-et, "at every time / at any time")
**EMET:** "at all times"
**LOGOS:** "at any time"
**PNEUMA:** "whenever he chooses"
**Ruling:** "at any time"
**Type:** Word choice / idiomatic rendering
**Notes:** Be-khol-et is a blanket temporal prohibition: "at all times = at no time except the prescribed one." LOGOS's "at any time" captures this in natural English. PNEUMA's "whenever he chooses" adds the interpretive element of Aaron's volition that is not in the Hebrew; the point is not Aaron's choice but the absolute nature of the prohibition.

---

## Disagreement 7 — V. 2: kapporet — "kapporet [atonement cover]" vs. "mercy seat"

**Verse:** 2, 13, 14, 15 (all occurrences)
**Hebrew:** הַכַּפֹּרֶת (ha-kapporet)
**EMET:** "the kapporet" (untranslated, no bracketed gloss)
**LOGOS:** "the kapporet [atonement cover]" (first occurrence); "the kapporet" thereafter
**PNEUMA:** "the mercy seat" throughout
**Ruling:** "the kapporet [atonement cover]" on first occurrence; "the kapporet" thereafter
**Type:** Technical term / project convention
**Notes:** This is the most significant single disagreement in the chapter. The project ruling (established in the chapter manifest) is to preserve kapporet untranslated with bracketed gloss. EMET preserves the transliteration but omits the gloss on first occurrence (departure from the convention). PNEUMA uses "mercy seat" throughout — the KJV/traditional rendering derived from Luther's Gnadenstuhl — which captures the grace/propitiation dimension but abandons the transliteration ruling. LOGOS follows the project convention exactly. The LXX uses ἱλαστήριον (hilasterion) throughout, a term with its own NT theological trajectory (Rom 3:25; Heb 9:5). The consensus adopts LOGOS's convention.

---

## Disagreement 8 — V. 2: "Holy Place" vs. "Most Holy Place" (PNEUMA)

**Verse:** 2 (and vv. 16, 17, 20, 23, 27)
**Hebrew:** הַקֹּדֶשׁ (ha-qodesh, "the holy place")
**EMET:** "the holy place"
**LOGOS:** "the Holy Place"
**PNEUMA:** "the Most Holy Place"
**Ruling:** "the Holy Place" in vv. 2, 16, 17, 20, 23; "Holy of Holies" reserved for v. 33 (miqdashe ha-qodesh)
**Type:** Technical term / precision
**Notes:** The MT in v. 2 uses ha-qodesh (the general "holy place / sanctuary"), not qodesh ha-qodashim ("holy of holies"). While context makes clear the innermost chamber is intended, the Hebrew text itself does not use the full "Holy of Holies" formula until v. 33. PNEUMA's "Most Holy Place" throughout collapses this distinction. LOGOS correctly renders ha-qodesh as "Holy Place" and reserves the fuller expression for where the text uses it. The distinction matters because ha-qodesh also refers to the broader sanctuary in some contexts (v. 16: making atonement "for the Holy Place" encompasses the outer room as well).

---

## Disagreement 9 — V. 2: "lest he die" vs. "that he not die" vs. "or he will die"

**Verse:** 2
**Hebrew:** וְלֹא יָמוּת (ve-lo' yamut, purpose clause: "so that he not die")
**EMET:** "that he not die"
**LOGOS:** "lest he die"
**PNEUMA:** "or he will die"
**Ruling:** "lest he die"
**Type:** Syntactic / purpose clause
**Notes:** The Hebrew is a purpose clause (result clause): "in order that he not die." LOGOS's "lest he die" captures this natural purpose expression. PNEUMA's "or he will die" converts it to a condition, slightly changing the register from purpose to threat. EMET's "that he not die" is accurate but stilted. The same formula recurs in v. 13; ruling applies to both.

---

## Disagreement 10 — V. 3: "But with this" vs. "But thus" vs. "This is how"

**Verse:** 3
**Hebrew:** בְּזֹאת (be-zot, "with/in this")
**EMET:** "With this"
**LOGOS:** "But thus"
**PNEUMA:** "This is how"
**Ruling:** "But with this"
**Type:** Connective / adversative
**Notes:** The verse opens with a contrastive pivot from the prohibition of v. 2 to the permitted mode of entry. LOGOS's "but thus" captures the adversative; EMET's "with this" captures the pointing-forward demonstrative; PNEUMA's "this is how" is paraphrastic. The consensus combines both elements.

---

## Disagreement 11 — V. 3: "a bull" vs. "a bull from the herd" vs. "a young bull"

**Verse:** 3
**Hebrew:** פַּר בֶּן-בָּקָר (par ben-baqar, "a bull, son of the herd")
**EMET:** "a bull"
**LOGOS:** "a bull from the herd"
**PNEUMA:** "a young bull"
**Ruling:** "a bull"
**Type:** Word choice / completeness
**Notes:** The full phrase par ben-baqar specifies both the species (par = bull) and its class (ben-baqar = "son of the herd" = a bovine, specifically a domesticated herd animal). LOGOS renders the full phrase. PNEUMA adds "young" as an age qualifier not in the MT. EMET's "a bull" is concise and recognizable. The consensus uses "a bull" for readability; the "from the herd" qualifier is contextually understood.

---

## Disagreement 12 — V. 4: "breeches" vs. "undergarments"

**Verse:** 4
**Hebrew:** מִכְנְסֵי-בָד (mikhnesei-bad, "undergarments/breeches of linen")
**EMET:** "linen breeches"
**LOGOS:** "linen undergarments"
**PNEUMA:** "linen undergarments"
**Ruling:** "linen undergarments"
**Type:** Word choice / archaic vocabulary
**Notes:** Mikhnasayim (plural) refers to the close-fitting linen garment worn against the body beneath the tunic (described in Exod 28:42 as covering "from the loins to the thighs"). EMET's "breeches" is accurate but archaic in contemporary English. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "undergarments," which is clearer.

---

## Disagreement 13 — V. 4: "sacred garments" vs. "holy garments"

**Verse:** 4
**Hebrew:** בִּגְדֵי-קֹדֶשׁ (bigdei qodesh, "garments of holiness / holy garments")
**EMET:** "holy garments"
**LOGOS:** "holy garments"
**PNEUMA:** "sacred garments"
**Ruling:** "holy garments"
**Type:** Word choice / project vocabulary
**Notes:** Qodesh is the project standard word rendered "holy" throughout Leviticus. PNEUMA's substitution of "sacred" for "holy" at this point is inconsistent with the established vocabulary. "Sacred" and "holy" are near-synonyms, but consistency is important in a translation that uses these terms as technical categories.

---

## Disagreement 14 — V. 4: Bathing instruction placement

**Verse:** 4
**Hebrew:** וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם אֶת-בְּשָׂרוֹ וּלְבֵשָׁם ("and he shall bathe his body in water and put them on" — at the end)
**EMET:** Bathing at end ("shall bathe his flesh in water and put them on")
**LOGOS:** Bathing at end ("He shall bathe his body in water and put them on")
**PNEUMA:** Bathing inverted to beginning ("Before putting them on, he shall bathe his whole body in water")
**Ruling:** Bathing at end, following Hebrew order
**Type:** Word order / structural
**Notes:** The Hebrew clearly places the bathing instruction at the end of the garment-donning sequence. PNEUMA inverts the order for logical clarity ("you bathe before dressing"), but this changes the structure of the Hebrew sentence. The MT order is preserved in consensus.

---

## Disagreement 15 — V. 5: "congregation" vs. "community"

**Verse:** 5
**Hebrew:** עֲדַת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (adat bney yisrael, "congregation of the sons of Israel")
**EMET:** "congregation of the sons of Yisra'el"
**LOGOS:** "congregation of the sons of Israel"
**PNEUMA:** "whole community of Israel"
**Ruling:** "congregation of the sons of Israel"
**Type:** Address formula / project vocabulary
**Notes:** PNEUMA makes two changes: (a) "congregation" → "community"; (b) drops "sons of." "Congregation" is the project standard for adat (cf. Lev 4:13, 8:3, 9:5 consensus rulings). "Community" is an acceptable modern equivalent but inconsistent with established usage. Dropping bney (sons of) flattens the generational-familial resonance.

---

## Disagreement 16 — V. 5: "take" vs. "receive"

**Verse:** 5
**Hebrew:** יִקַּח (yiqqach, "he shall take")
**EMET:** "he shall take"
**LOGOS:** "he shall take"
**PNEUMA:** "he shall receive"
**Ruling:** "he shall take"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Laqach/yiqqach ("to take") in priestly contexts means to procure/acquire the offering material. PNEUMA's "receive" implies a passive handover; the MT does not specify who presents the animals to Aaron. "Take" is more accurate.

---

## Disagreement 17 — V. 6: "present" vs. "bring near" vs. "offer" vs. "bring"

**Verse:** 6 (and vv. 9, 11)
**Hebrew:** וְהִקְרִיב (ve-hiqriv, Hiphil of qarav)
**EMET:** "bring near" (v. 6), "bring near" (v. 9, 11)
**LOGOS:** "offer" (v. 6), "present" (v. 9, 11)
**PNEUMA:** "bring" (v. 6), "offer" (v. 9)
**Ruling:** "present"
**Type:** Technical sacrificial term
**Notes:** The Hiphil of qarav ("to cause to draw near / to present before") is the standard technical verb for presenting an offering. "Bring near" (EMET) is literal but sounds odd in English. "Offer" (LOGOS v. 6, PNEUMA v. 9) collapses the two-stage process (presenting + then slaughtering/offering). "Present" is precise: it captures the act of bringing the animal before the LORD without anticipating the slaughter. LOGOS uses "present" in v. 9 and 11; consensus extends "present" consistently.

---

## Disagreement 18 — V. 7: "set them" vs. "stand them" vs. "present them"

**Verse:** 7
**Hebrew:** וְהֶעֱמִיד (ve-he'emid, Hiphil of 'amad, "to cause to stand / to station")
**EMET:** "he shall stand them"
**LOGOS:** "set them"
**PNEUMA:** "present them"
**Ruling:** "set them"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** He'emid means to place/station (something living) upright — causing the goats to stand before the LORD. EMET's "stand them" is literal but awkward in English. LOGOS's "set them" is natural. PNEUMA's "present them" comes too close to the hiqriv verb of v. 6. "Set them before the LORD" is the ruling.

---

## Disagreement 19 — V. 10: "sending it away to Azazel" vs. "sent off into the wilderness to Azazel"

**Verse:** 10
**Hebrew:** לְשַׁלַּח אֹתוֹ לַעֲזָאזֵל הַמִּדְבָּרָה (leshalach oto la-Azazel ha-midbarah, "to send it to Azazel, toward the wilderness")
**EMET:** "to send it away as Azazel to the wilderness"
**LOGOS:** "by sending it away to Azazel into the wilderness"
**PNEUMA:** "and then sent off into the wilderness to Azazel"
**Ruling:** "by sending it away to Azazel into the wilderness"
**Type:** Syntactic / word order
**Notes:** The Hebrew places Azazel before "the wilderness" (ha-midbarah = directional: "toward/into the wilderness"). EMET's "as Azazel" is incorrect — the goat is not being sent "as" Azazel but "to/for" Azazel (la-Azazel is the same prepositional phrase as in v. 8). LOGOS's syntax is the most accurate. PNEUMA reverses the order (wilderness before Azazel). Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## Disagreement 20 — V. 12: "coals of fire" vs. "fiery coals" vs. "burning coals"

**Verse:** 12
**Hebrew:** גַּחֲלֵי אֵשׁ (gachalei esh, "coals of fire")
**EMET:** "fiery coals"
**LOGOS:** "coals of fire"
**PNEUMA:** "burning coals"
**Ruling:** "coals of fire"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Gachalei esh is a construct phrase: "coals [construct] of fire." LOGOS preserves the construct exactly. EMET's "fiery coals" and PNEUMA's "burning coals" are both idiomatic but interpret rather than translate the Hebrew construct. "Coals of fire" is more literal and evocative.

---

## Disagreement 21 — V. 12: "his hands full" vs. "two handfuls"

**Verse:** 12
**Hebrew:** מְלֹא חָפְנָיו (melo' hofnav, "fulness of his palms")
**EMET:** "his hands full"
**LOGOS:** "his hands full"
**PNEUMA:** "two handfuls"
**Ruling:** "his hands full"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Hofnayim is the dual noun ("two palms/handfuls") from which melo' hofnav derives; the dual is absorbed into "his hands full" in natural English. PNEUMA's "two handfuls" is technically accurate (hofnayim = dual) but "his hands full" is the idiomatic equivalent and avoids the implication of a measured unit.

---

## Disagreement 22 — V. 12 and 15: "inside the veil" vs. "behind the curtain"

**Verse:** 12, 15
**Hebrew:** לִפְנִים אֶל-הַפָּרֹכֶת / מִבֵּית לַפָּרֹכֶת (lifnim el-ha-parokhet / mi-beit la-parokhet)
**EMET:** "from within to the veil"
**LOGOS:** "inside the veil"
**PNEUMA:** "behind the curtain"
**Ruling:** "inside the veil"
**Type:** Technical term / spatial vocabulary
**Notes:** The parokhet is the heavy woven veil/screen that divides the Holy Place from the Holy of Holies (Exod 26:31–35). The Hebrew spatial phrase "inside the veil" or "within the veil" specifies the sanctum beyond it. PNEUMA's "behind the curtain" is spatially accurate but "curtain" understates the parokhet; this is not a light curtain but a massive woven barrier. LOGOS's "inside the veil" is the established Levitical rendering and clearest in English.

---

## Disagreement 23 — V. 13: "the testimony" vs. "the covenant tablets"

**Verse:** 13
**Hebrew:** הָעֵדֻת (ha-edut, "the testimony/witness")
**EMET:** "the testimony"
**LOGOS:** "the testimony"
**PNEUMA:** "the covenant tablets"
**Ruling:** "the testimony"
**Type:** Technical term
**Notes:** Ha-edut ("the testimony") is the established name for the two stone tablets stored in the ark (Exod 25:16, 21; 31:18; 40:20). The full phrase aron ha-edut ("ark of the testimony") is a fixed term throughout the Pentateuch. PNEUMA's "covenant tablets" is interpretively accurate but introduces "covenant" (berit) which is a distinct Hebrew word not present here. The ark is the ark of the testimony, not the ark of the covenant, in this specific phrase.

---

## Disagreement 24 — V. 13: "lest he die" vs. "otherwise he will die"

**Verse:** 13
**Hebrew:** וְלֹא יָמוּת (ve-lo' yamut — same formula as v. 2)
**EMET:** "so that he not die"
**LOGOS:** "lest he die"
**PNEUMA:** "otherwise he will die"
**Ruling:** "lest he die"
**Type:** Syntactic / purpose clause
**Notes:** Same ruling as Disagreement 9 (v. 2). The clause is a purpose clause, not a conditional. "Lest he die" is the consensus rendering.

---

## Disagreement 25 — V. 16: "uncleannesses" vs. "ritual impurities"

**Verse:** 16 (and v. 19)
**Hebrew:** טֻמְאֹת (tum'ot, "uncleannesses")
**EMET:** "uncleannesses"
**LOGOS:** "uncleannesses"
**PNEUMA:** "ritual impurities"
**Ruling:** "uncleannesses"
**Type:** Technical term / project vocabulary
**Notes:** The project convention (established in Lev 11–15 series) renders tameh/tum'ah as "unclean/uncleanness." PNEUMA adds the qualifier "ritual" to distinguish ritual from moral impurity. The MT itself does not make this lexical distinction at the word level — tum'ah covers both domains. Adding "ritual" narrows the term. "Uncleannesses" is the ruling.

---

## Disagreement 26 — V. 16: "transgressions" vs. "acts of rebellion"

**Verse:** 16 (and v. 21)
**Hebrew:** פְּשָׁעִים / פֶּשַׁע (pesha'im / pesha, "transgressions/rebellions")
**EMET:** "transgressions"
**LOGOS:** "transgressions"
**PNEUMA:** "acts of rebellion"
**Ruling:** "transgressions"
**Type:** Word choice / project vocabulary
**Notes:** Pesha denotes deliberate, volitional violation — rebellion against authority, not mere failure. PNEUMA's "acts of rebellion" accurately captures this willful dimension. However, "transgressions" is the established project rendering for pesha throughout the Leviticus series (cf. earlier chapters) and is the standard formal-equivalence choice (ESV, NASB, KJV tradition). Consistency with the established term is the ruling factor.

---

## Disagreement 27 — V. 17: "no man" vs. "no one"

**Verse:** 17
**Hebrew:** וְכָל-אָדָם לֹא יִהְיֶה (ve-khol-adam lo' yihyeh, "and no man/person shall be")
**EMET:** "no man"
**LOGOS:** "no man"
**PNEUMA:** "no one"
**Ruling:** "No man"
**Type:** Word choice / generic humanity
**Notes:** Adam is the generic term for human being in Hebrew (not the specifically masculine ish). Both EMET and LOGOS render the traditional "no man" as a generic. PNEUMA's "no one" is more inclusive in English. The project has consistently rendered adam as "man" (generic) and ish as "man" (masculine) throughout Leviticus for consistency with the legal register of the text. "No man" as the generic is the ruling.

---

## Disagreement 28 — V. 17: "congregation" vs. "assembly" vs. "assembled community"

**Verse:** 17
**Hebrew:** כָּל-קְהַל יִשְׂרָאֵל (kol-qehal yisrael, "all the congregation/assembly of Israel")
**EMET:** "congregation of Yisra'el"
**LOGOS:** "assembly of Israel"
**PNEUMA:** "whole assembled community of Israel"
**Ruling:** "congregation of Israel"
**Type:** Technical term
**Notes:** Qahal ("assembly, congregation") is the standard term for Israel's formal gathering. LOGOS uses "assembly" in v. 17 (departing from its own use of "congregation" in v. 5 for adat). PNEUMA's "whole assembled community" is an expansion. The project standard is "congregation" for both adat and qahal in Leviticus for consistency; where the terms differ theologically in context this is noted, but in the consensus text "congregation" is the ruling term.

---

## Disagreement 29 — V. 21: "a man who is in readiness" vs. "a man appointed for the task"

**Verse:** 21
**Hebrew:** בְּיַד אִישׁ עִתִּי (be-yad ish itti, "by the hand of a man [of readiness/at hand]")
**EMET:** "by the hand of a ready man" (implied in the full text)
**LOGOS:** "by the hand of a man who is in readiness"
**PNEUMA:** "in the care of a man appointed for the task"
**Ruling:** "by the hand of a man who is in readiness"
**Type:** Hapax legomenon / disputed term
**Notes:** Itti is a hapax legomenon. Possible meanings: "ready, at hand, designated, timely." LOGOS follows the LXX (ἑτοίμου ἀνθρώπου, "a ready man") and preserves the ambiguity. PNEUMA's "appointed for the task" goes beyond the text — the MT does not say he was appointed in advance for this specific task, only that he is someone ready/at hand. EMET and LOGOS agree on the readiness sense. Consensus: LOGOS.

---

## Disagreement 30 — V. 22: "a land cut off" vs. "a remote land" vs. "a desolate place"

**Verse:** 22
**Hebrew:** אֶרֶץ גְּזֵרָה (eretz gezeirah, "a land of cutting-off / a cut-off land")
**EMET:** "a land cut off"
**LOGOS:** "a remote land"
**PNEUMA:** "a desolate place"
**Ruling:** "a land cut off"
**Type:** Word choice / key image
**Notes:** Gezeirah comes from gazar ("to cut, to cut off, to separate"). The phrase means "a land that is severed from the inhabited world" — not merely remote (LOGOS) or desolate (PNEUMA) but actively cut off, amputated from the community. EMET's "a land cut off" preserves the root image of severance. This matters theologically: the sins are not merely sent somewhere far away but to a place that is by definition beyond the boundary of the inhabited, ordered world. PNEUMA's "desolate place" loses the active sense of severance; LOGOS's "remote" loses it entirely. Ruling: EMET.

---

## Disagreement 31 — V. 24: "his garments" vs. "regular garments"

**Verse:** 24
**Hebrew:** בְּגָדָיו (bigdav, "his garments")
**EMET:** "his garments"
**LOGOS:** "his garments"
**PNEUMA:** "his regular garments"
**Ruling:** "his garments"
**Type:** Addition / interpretive gloss
**Notes:** PNEUMA adds "regular" to distinguish Aaron's regular priestly garments from the special linen garments of vv. 3–4. The distinction is real and contextually clear; adding "regular" is a helpful clarification but it is not in the Hebrew. The consensus does not add interpretive glosses not present in the MT.

---

## Disagreement 32 — V. 26: "the one who sends away the goat for Azazel" vs. "he who releases" vs. "the man who released the Azazel goat"

**Verse:** 26
**Hebrew:** וְהַמְשַׁלֵּחַ אֶת-הַשָּׂעִיר לַעֲזָאזֵל (ve-ha-meshallach et-ha-sa'ir la-Azazel)
**EMET:** "the one who sends away the goat for Azazel"
**LOGOS:** "he who releases the goat for Azazel"
**PNEUMA:** "the man who released the Azazel goat"
**Ruling:** "the one who sends away the goat for Azazel"
**Type:** Word choice / participial phrase
**Notes:** Ha-meshallach is the Piel participle of shalach ("to send away / to dispatch / to release"). EMET preserves "sends away" (the key Piel sense of dispatching). LOGOS uses "releases" — acceptable but the Piel of shalach emphasizes dispatching, not merely letting go. PNEUMA's "the Azazel goat" as a compound is colloquial and not in the Hebrew syntax. Consensus: EMET's rendering is the most precise.

---

## Disagreement 33 — V. 27: "skin" vs. "hides"

**Verse:** 27
**Hebrew:** אֶת-עֹרֹתָם (et-orotam, "their skins/hides")
**EMET:** "their skins"
**LOGOS:** "their skin"
**PNEUMA:** "their hides"
**Ruling:** "their skin"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Minor variant. Or means skin (of an animal). "Hides" (PNEUMA) is accurate but suggests prepared leather; "skin" in the context of an unprocessed carcass being burned is more natural. LOGOS's singular collective "their skin" is adopted.

---

## Disagreement 34 — V. 29: "a statute forever" vs. "a perpetual statute" vs. "a permanent statute"

**Verse:** 29 (and vv. 31, 34)
**Hebrew:** לְחֻקַּת עוֹלָם (le-chuqat olam, "as a statute of eternity / forever")
**EMET:** "a perpetual statute"
**LOGOS:** "a statute forever"
**PNEUMA:** "a permanent statute"
**Ruling:** "a statute forever"
**Type:** Formula rendering
**Notes:** Le-chuqat olam appears three times in this chapter (vv. 29, 31, 34), establishing the permanent statutory character of Yom Kippur. Olam ("eternity, forever, everlasting") is most directly rendered "forever" (LOGOS). "Perpetual" (EMET) and "permanent" (PNEUMA) are near-synonyms but "forever" best captures the temporal depth of olam. Consistency across the three occurrences is important; LOGOS is adopted uniformly.

---

## Disagreement 35 — V. 29: "afflict yourselves" vs. "humble yourselves"

**Verse:** 29 (and v. 31)
**Hebrew:** וְעִנִּיתֶם אֶת-נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם (ve-innitem et-nafshoteikhem, "you shall afflict your souls/selves")
**EMET:** "you shall afflict your souls"
**LOGOS:** "you shall afflict yourselves"
**PNEUMA:** "you shall humble yourselves"
**Ruling:** "you shall afflict yourselves"
**Type:** Key theological term / Yom Kippur self-denial formula
**Notes:** This is the chapter's most important repeated term in the statutory section. The Piel of anah II (anah, "to afflict, to oppress, to humble") with the direct object et-nafshoteikhem ("your souls/selves") is the technical Levitical formula for Yom Kippur self-denial. EMET's "your souls" preserves nefesh as a distinct object. LOGOS's "yourselves" is the natural reflexive English rendering. PNEUMA follows the LXX (ταπεινώσετε τὰς ψυχὰς ὑμῶν, "you shall humble your souls") in rendering it as "humble yourselves." The LXX choice of ταπεινόω ("to humble") emphasizes the interior/attitudinal dimension; the MT anah encompasses bodily self-denial (fasting, refraining from bathing, anointing, leather sandals, marital relations — all enumerated in b.Yoma 73b). "Afflict" preserves the full bodily-spiritual range; "humble" narrows it to the interior. Consensus: "afflict yourselves" (LOGOS).

---

## Disagreement 36 — V. 29: "the native and the sojourner" vs. "the native-born... the foreigner"

**Verse:** 29
**Hebrew:** הָאֶזְרָח וְהַגֵּר (ha-ezrach ve-ha-ger)
**EMET:** "the native and the sojourner"
**LOGOS:** "the native and the sojourner"
**PNEUMA:** "the native-born nor the foreigner"
**Ruling:** "the native and the sojourner"
**Type:** Technical term
**Notes:** Ger is the "resident alien" — a non-Israelite living within Israel's community under Israel's laws. This is a carefully defined legal category, distinct from both the native Israelite (ezrach) and the complete outsider (nokri). PNEUMA's "foreigner" overstates the distance; a ger is not merely a foreigner but a resident with legal obligations and protections. "Sojourner" (EMET, LOGOS) preserves this nuance and is the project standard term throughout Leviticus. The LXX renders ὁ αὐτόχθων καὶ ὁ προσήλυτος ("the native-born and the proselyte") — an interesting rendering that in later usage acquires the sense of a religious convert; the MT does not imply conversion.

---

## Disagreement 37 — V. 31: "sabbath of solemn rest" vs. "sabbath of complete rest"

**Verse:** 31
**Hebrew:** שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן (shabbat shabbaton)
**EMET:** "a sabbath of solemn rest" (plus transliteration in notes)
**LOGOS:** "a sabbath of solemn rest [shabbat shabbaton]"
**PNEUMA:** "a sabbath of complete rest"
**Ruling:** "a sabbath of solemn rest [shabbat shabbaton]"
**Type:** Technical term / superlative Sabbath formula
**Notes:** Shabbat shabbaton is the doubled intensive Sabbath formula, appearing in the MT only for Yom Kippur (Lev 16:31; 23:32) and the weekly Sabbath (Exod 31:15; 35:2; Lev 23:3) — the highest Sabbath grade in the Levitical calendar. "Solemn rest" (EMET, LOGOS) is the established English rendering (ESV/NASB tradition), conveying both the cessation and the weightiness. "Complete rest" (PNEUMA) emphasizes the totality of cessation but loses the "solemn" dimension. LOGOS preserves the transliteration in brackets per project convention. Ruling: LOGOS.

---

## Disagreement 38 — V. 32: "consecrated to serve as priest" vs. "ordained to serve"

**Verse:** 32
**Hebrew:** וּמִלֵּא אֶת-יָדוֹ לְכַהֵן (u-milleh et-yado lekhahein, "who fills his hand to serve as priest" — ordination idiom)
**EMET:** "consecrated to minister as priest"
**LOGOS:** "consecrated to serve as priest"
**PNEUMA:** "ordained to serve"
**Ruling:** "consecrated to serve as priest"
**Type:** Technical idiom / ordination formula
**Notes:** Milleh et-yado ("to fill his hand") is the standard Hebrew idiom for priestly ordination (cf. Lev 8:33; Exod 28:41; 29:9). LOGOS renders "consecrated to serve as priest" — capturing the ordination concept with the specific priestly-service phrase. PNEUMA's "ordained" is the functional equivalent but abandons the specific consecration-language. The phrase "in his father's place" (tachat aviv) establishes dynastic succession: the high-priestly role is perpetuated by Aaron's descendants.

---

## Disagreement 39 — V. 33: "holy sanctuary" vs. "Most Holy Place"

**Verse:** 33
**Hebrew:** מִקְדַּשׁ הַקֹּדֶשׁ (miqdashe ha-qodesh, "the sanctuary of holiness / the holy sanctuary")
**EMET:** "the holy sanctuary"
**LOGOS:** "the holy sanctuary"
**PNEUMA:** "the Most Holy Place"
**Ruling:** "the holy sanctuary"
**Type:** Technical term / precision
**Notes:** Miqdashe ha-qodesh in v. 33 refers to the sanctuary complex as a whole ("the holy sanctuary") — the verse then proceeds to list the tent of meeting and the altar as additional items alongside it, indicating the first item encompasses the inner sanctum specifically. PNEUMA's "Most Holy Place" is too narrow; the term in v. 33 covers the innermost chamber in its role as the supremely holy part of the whole sanctuary. "The holy sanctuary" (EMET, LOGOS) is the correct rendering.

---

## Disagreement 40 — V. 34: "And Moses did" vs. "And it was done"

**Verse:** 34
**Hebrew:** וַיַּעַשׂ כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת-מֹשֶׁה (va-ya'as ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh, "and he did as the LORD commanded Moses")
**EMET:** "and he did as the LORD commanded Moshe"
**LOGOS:** "and Moses did as the LORD commanded him"
**PNEUMA:** "And it was done as the LORD commanded Moses"
**Ruling:** "And Moses did as the LORD commanded him"
**Type:** Closing formula / subject identification
**Notes:** The subject of va-ya'as is implicit in the Hebrew (the verb has no stated subject after v. 34's commands). LOGOS makes Moses the subject of the closing formula, consistent with the standard priestly-narrative close (cf. Exod 40:16; Lev 8:36; Num 27:22: "and Moses did as the LORD commanded"). PNEUMA's passive "it was done" removes the personal subject. EMET's "he did" preserves the implicit subject without naming it. LOGOS's explicit "Moses did" follows the closest parallels in the priestly tradition and is the most natural completion of the formula.

---

## Disagreement 41 — V. 30: "atonement shall be made" vs. "atonement will be made"

**Verse:** 30
**Hebrew:** יְכַפֵּר עֲלֵיכֶם (yekhapper aleikhem, "he/it shall make atonement for you")
**EMET:** "atonement shall be made for you"
**LOGOS:** "atonement shall be made for you"
**PNEUMA:** "atonement will be made for you"
**Ruling:** "atonement shall be made for you"
**Type:** Modal register
**Notes:** The imperfect yekhapper in a statutory context carries modal force: "shall be made" (promise-obligation) rather than merely "will be made" (future prediction). EMET and LOGOS preserve "shall." PNEUMA's "will" is less specifically promissory. This is a declaration of divine commitment to atone on this day; "shall" is the stronger promise form.

---

## Disagreement 42 — V. 16 LXX variant: Three-term catalogue reduced to one

**Verse:** 16
**Hebrew (MT):** טֻמְאֹת... וּמִפִּשְׁעֵיהֶם לְכָל-חַטֹּאתָם (tum'ot... pesha'im... chatot — three terms)
**LXX (Vaticanus):** ἀπὸ πασῶν τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν αὐτῶν ("from all their sins" — one term)
**MT Rendering (all three agents):** Three terms preserved
**Ruling:** Three-term distinction preserved (MT followed)
**Type:** LXX variant / textual
**Notes:** The LXX Vaticanus in v. 16 reduces the MT's three-term sin catalogue (uncleannesses, transgressions, sins) to a single Greek phrase "from all their sins." This is a genuine simplification by the LXX translator — likely to avoid the complexity of distinguishing three Greek equivalents. All three agents follow the MT's three-term structure; the LXX variant is noted for the record. The MT preserves a deliberate comprehensive taxonomy of sin-types. The LXX does restore the three-term structure in v. 21, suggesting the v. 16 reduction is not a text-form variant but a translational choice.

---

*SOPHIA disagreement log complete for Leviticus 16. Total: 42 disagreements across 34 verses.*
